title: W3 B Continuity
body:
Mathematical Economics

     Juan Carlos Carbajal

  School of Economics UNSW–Sydney



  Week 3B    —    Term 1, 2026




                                    1/18
                  Existence of Solution to the UMP


Recall the consumer’s utility maximization problem. First order of
business is to make sure it can be solved.

 Proposition 1

   Given a continuous utility function u : X → R, the consumer’s
   utility maximization problem (UMP)
                  max u(x)       subject to   p·x ≤ m
                  x≥0


   has at least one solution.


Existence of solutions to optimal behavior problems relies (in part) on the
continuity properties of functions.




                                                                              2/18
 Continuity of Real-Valued Functions Defined on R




                                                   3/18
                        Continuity of a Function


Intuitively, f is continuous at a point x̂ if we can get arbitrarily close to
the value f (x̂) by getting sufficiently close to x̂.

                             f (x)



                                                         f

                         f (x̂)



                                                             x
                                     x̂




                                                                                4/18
                        Continuity of a Function


Intuitively, f is continuous at a point x̂ if we can get arbitrarily close to
the value f (x̂) by getting sufficiently close to x̂.

                             f (x)



                                                         f

                         f (x̂)



                                                             x
                                     x̂




                                                                                4/18
                       Continuity of a Function



 Definition 1

   Let I ⊆ R be an open interval and f : I → R.
    (a) f is continuous at x̂ ∈ I if for every ϵ > 0 there exists some
        δ > 0 such that for all x ∈ I,
                  |x − x̂| < δ     =⇒     |f (x) − f (x̂)| < ϵ.




Remark
In the above definition, δ > 0 may depend on ϵ and on x̂.




                                                                         5/18
                         Continuity of a Function



 Definition 1

   Let I ⊆ R be an open interval and f : I → R.
    (a) f is continuous at x̂ ∈ I if for every ϵ > 0 there exists some
        δ > 0 such that for all x ∈ I,
                  |x − x̂| < δ     =⇒     |f (x) − f (x̂)| < ϵ.

    (b) f is a continuous function if it is continuous at every point on
        its domain I.


Remark
In the above definition, δ > 0 may depend on ϵ and on x̂.




                                                                           5/18
                               Examples

Example 1

  (a) f1 (x) = α x is continuous on I1 = R.

                             Examples

Example 1

 (a) f1 (x) = α x is continuous on I1 = R.
              √
 (b) f2 (x) = x is continuous on I2 = (0, +∞).

  (c) f3 defined on I3 = (−1, 1) by
                                   

                         f3 (x) =
                                   1   for x > 0

     is discontinuous at x̂ = 0, but continuous everywhere else.

                             Examples

Example 1

 (a) f1 (x) = α x is continuous on I1 = R.
              √
 (b) f2 (x) = x is continuous on I2 = (0, +∞).

  (c) f3 defined on I3 = (−1, 1) by
                                   
                                   0   for x ≤ 0,
                         f3 (x) =
                                   1   for x > 0

     is discontinuous at x̂ = 0, but continuous everywhere else.

 (d) What about f4 defined on I4 = R by
                             
                             0 for x rational,
                   f4 (x) =
                             1 for x irrational.




                                                                   6/18
                                Example 3 (a)

If α = 0, then f1 (x) = 0 everywhere — in this case f1 is clearly continuous.
In general, a constant function is continuous.

Assume that α ̸= 0. To show that f1 is continuous at x̂ ∈ R, fix ϵ > 0.




                                                                                7/18
                                Example 3 (a)

If α = 0, then f1 (x) = 0 everywhere — in this case f1 is clearly continuous.
In general, a constant function is continuous.

Assume that α ̸= 0. To show that f1 is continuous at x̂ ∈ R, fix ϵ > 0.
                                ϵ
  ◦ Let δ ∈ R be such that δ = |α| > 0.

  ◦ If x ∈ R is such that |x − x̂| < δ, we obtain

                       |f1 (x) − f1 (x̂)| = |α x − α x̂|
                                         = |α| |x − x̂|




                                                                                7/18
                                Example 3 (a)

If α = 0, then f1 (x) = 0 everywhere — in this case f1 is clearly continuous.
In general, a constant function is continuous.

Assume that α ̸= 0. To show that f1 is continuous at x̂ ∈ R, fix ϵ > 0.
                                ϵ
  ◦ Let δ ∈ R be such that δ = |α| > 0.

  ◦ If x ∈ R is such that |x − x̂| < δ, we obtain

                       |f1 (x) − f1 (x̂)| = |α x − α x̂|
                                         = |α| |x − x̂|
                                         < |α| δ = ϵ.




                                                                                7/18
                                Example 3 (a)

If α = 0, then f1 (x) = 0 everywhere — in this case f1 is clearly continuous.
In general, a constant function is continuous.

Assume that α ̸= 0. To show that f1 is continuous at x̂ ∈ R, fix ϵ > 0.
                                ϵ
  ◦ Let δ ∈ R be such that δ = |α| > 0.

  ◦ If x ∈ R is such that |x − x̂| < δ, we obtain

                       |f1 (x) − f1 (x̂)| = |α x − α x̂|
                                         = |α| |x − x̂|
                                         < |α| δ = ϵ.

  ◦ It follows that f1 is continuous at x̂.

  ◦ Since x̂ was arbitrarily chosen, f1 is continuous on I1 = R.

                        √
To show that f2 (x) =       x is continuous at arbitrary x̂ > 0, fix ϵ > 0.
  ◦ Let δ ∈ R be such that δ = ? > 0.

  ◦ For any x > 0 satisfying |x − x̂| < δ, we have

                         δ      δ

                       x + x̂    x̂

  ◦ Our desired δ > 0 is δ = ϵ         x̂.

  ◦ Hence f2 is continuous at x̂ > 0, and thus continuous on I2 = (0, +∞).




                                                                              8/18
                                  Example 3 (c)



f3 : (−1, 1) → R is defined by
                                     
                                     0   for x ≤ 0,
                          f3 (x) =
                                     1   for x > 0.

To show that f3 is continuous at any x̂ < 0, fix ϵ > 0.
  ◦ Choose δ = 21 |x̂| > 0.

  ◦ For any x ∈ (−1, 1), if |x − x̂| < δ then we have

                       x − x̂ ≤ |x − x̂| < δ = − 21 x̂.

  ◦ This means that x < 12 x̂ < 0.

  ◦ Thus |f3 (x) − f3 (x̂)| = 0 < ϵ.




                                                          9/18
                                  Example 3 (c)




f3 : (−1, 1) → R is defined by
                                        
                                        0   for x ≤ 0,
                          f3 (x) =
                                        1   for x > 0.

To show that f3 is discontinuous at x̂ = 0, we are required to find some
ϵ > 0 for which the definition fails.
  ◦ Choose ϵ = 12 > 0.

  ◦ For any δ > 0, the δ–interval at x̂ = 0 includes strictly positive reals.

  ◦ Take any such real number: 0 < x < δ.

  ◦ Then |f3 (x) − f3 (x̂)| = |1 − 0| = 1 > 21 = ϵ.




                                                                                10/18
               Application: Continuous Preferences


There is only one composite good in the economy.
Anne has preferences represented by a continuous utility u : R → R.
  ◦ Suppose Anne strictly prefers to consume x to y.
    Note we are not saying that x > y. Anne may not be greedy.

  ◦ Since her preferences are continuous, consuming slightly more or less
    than x should also be strictly preferred than consuming y.

 Proposition 2

   If Anne’s utility function u : R → R is continuous and u(x) > u(y),
   then there exists some δ > 0 such that for all x′ ∈ (x − δ, x + δ), one
   has u(x′ ) > u(y).




                                                                             11/18
                Application: Continuous Preferences

Before proving Proposition 2, recall that for any real number a and any ϵ > 0,
                   |a| < ϵ   if and only if   − ϵ < a < ϵ.                   (1)




                                                                                   12/18
                Application: Continuous Preferences

Before proving Proposition 2, recall that for any real number a and any ϵ > 0,
                   |a| < ϵ   if and only if   − ϵ < a < ϵ.                   (1)


Proof    Fix ϵ = u(x) − u(y) > 0.
  ◦ Since u is continuous at x, there is some δ > 0 such that for all
    x′ ∈ (x − δ, x + δ), one has |u(x′ ) − u(x)| < ϵ.

  ◦ For any such x′ , using Equation (1) we can write

                          − ϵ < u(x′ ) − u(x) < ϵ.

  ◦ Replacing −ϵ = u(y) − u(x) above obtains

                          u(y) − u(x) < u(x′ ) − u(x).

  ◦ The result follows.




                                                                                   12/18
 Intermediate Value Theorem




                             13/18
                    Continuity on a Closed Interval


So far we stated continuity for functions defined on an open interval I ⊆ R.
In economic problems, the domain often has boundaries (e.g. x ≥ 0). It is
natural to consider a closed interval [a, b].




                                                                               14/18
                    Continuity on a Closed Interval


So far we stated continuity for functions defined on an open interval I ⊆ R.
In economic problems, the domain often has boundaries (e.g. x ≥ 0). It is
natural to consider a closed interval [a, b].


 Definition 2

    Let a < b and let f : [a, b] → R.
    (a) f is continuous at x̂ ∈ [a, b] if for every ϵ > 0 there exists some
         δ > 0 such that for all x ∈ [a, b],

                       |x − x̂| < δ =⇒ |f (x) − f (x̂)| < ϵ.

    (b) f is a continuous function if it is continuous at every point of
         its domain [a, b].




                                                                               14/18
                Endpoints and One-Sided Continuity




Let f : [a, b] → R and consider continuity of f at x̂.
  ◦ If x̂ ∈ (a, b), the idea is exactly the same as before: we want to get close
    to f (x̂) by approaching x̂.

  ◦ If x̂ = a, then x ∈ [a, b] forces x ≥ a, so we are only allowed to approach
    a from the right.

  ◦ If x̂ = b, then x ∈ [a, b] forces x ≤ b, so we are only allowed to approach
    b from the left.




                                                                                   15/18
                      Intermediate Value Theorem


 Proposition 3

    Let f : [a, b] → R be continuous. Take any number y ∈ R satisfying

                 min{f (a), f (b)} ≤ y ≤ max{f (a), f (b)}.

    Then there exists some c ∈ [a, b] such that f (c) = y.


The IVT formalizes the idea that a continuous function f cannot “jump over”
any value in its range.

Key Ideas
  ◦ Domain: a closed interval [a, b] — no gaps in the domain.
  ◦ Function: f continuous on [a, b] — continuity ensures no gaps in the
    range as well.


                                                                              16/18
          Intermediate Value Theorem: Representation




              f (x)

              f (b)


                y


              f (a)

                       a               c            b     x

Continuity ensures the graph must cross the blue horizontal line somewhere
between a and b.




                                                                             17/18
                         Economic Application



The IVT is a simple existence tool for one-dimensional equilibrium problems.

Existence of Market Clearing Price
  ◦ Let pL ≤ p ≤ pH denote the price of a good.

  ◦ Let D(p) and S(p) be the demand and supply functions.

  ◦ The excess demand function is z(p) = D(p) − S(p).




                                                                               18/18
                          Economic Application



The IVT is a simple existence tool for one-dimensional equilibrium problems.

Existence of Market Clearing Price
  ◦ Let pL ≤ p ≤ pH denote the price of a good.

  ◦ Let D(p) and S(p) be the demand and supply functions.

  ◦ The excess demand function is z(p) = D(p) − S(p).

  ◦ Reasonable assumptions:
      ◦ z(pL ) > 0 [shortage at lowest price];
      ◦ z(pH ) < 0 [surplus at highest price].




                                                                               18/18
                          Economic Application



The IVT is a simple existence tool for one-dimensional equilibrium problems.

Existence of Market Clearing Price
  ◦ Let pL ≤ p ≤ pH denote the price of a good.

  ◦ Let D(p) and S(p) be the demand and supply functions.

  ◦ The excess demand function is z(p) = D(p) − S(p).

  ◦ Reasonable assumptions:
      ◦ z(pL ) > 0 [shortage at lowest price];
      ◦ z(pH ) < 0 [surplus at highest price].

  ◦ If z is continuous,then there exists p∗ ∈ (pL , pH ) such that

              z(p∗ ) = 0,       or equivalently   D(p∗ ) = S(p∗ ).
